title: Why Fractals Create Infinite Detail From Simple Rules

lesson.learn:
Fractals are shapes that repeat similar patterns at different scales. A simple rule, applied again and again, can produce structures with enormous complexity. The Mandelbrot set, fractal trees, and Koch snowflakes all show how repetition can generate beauty that feels almost impossible.

Fractals matter because they appear in mathematics, nature, art, and computer graphics. They reveal how complexity can grow out of surprisingly simple rules.
lesson.simpler: Fractals are patterns that repeat over and over at different sizes. A few simple rules can create very complex shapes.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Fractals show that repeated simple rules can produce endless visual complexity.
lesson.deeperDive:
A fractal is not just a pretty pattern. It is often a way of describing systems that are self-similar or recursive. Coastlines, branching trees, clouds, and river networks often show fractal-like behavior because nature reuses similar branching logic over and over. In mathematics, that repetition can be extended indefinitely, which is why fractals can have detail at every scale.

The bigger idea is that order and complexity are not opposites. Sometimes the most intricate structures are produced by the simplest rules run many times.
lesson.funFact: Fractals can keep revealing detail when you zoom in.
references: Scientific American - Mandelbrot and fractal geometry

Recursion - a process repeated using its own output
Iteration - applying a rule over and over
Mandelbrot set - a famous fractal in complex dynamics
Complexity - rich detail emerging from simple rules
